name: Bemis Stream Prospect
short_description: This gentle slope prospect in western Maine offers opportunities to find quartz and feldspar specimens typical of the region's Appalachian geology. A lightly visited site with access via stream dra...
description:
Bemis Stream Prospect is located in the rugged mountains of Franklin County, Maine, at approximately 1,659 feet elevation. The area sits along stream drainages that have naturally exposed mineral-rich bedrock typical of the White Mountain zone of the Appalachian orogen. Collectors here can expect to find quartz specimens, feldspar crystals, and mica flakes weathered from pegmatite and granite outcrops common to western Maine.

The geology of this region reflects Ordovician to Devonian metamorphic and igneous activity that created the granite and pegmatite bodies found throughout the western Maine highlands. Stream erosion over millennia has concentrated minerals in alluvial deposits and along exposed banks, making Bemis Stream an attractive prospect for patient rockhounders.

Access to this prospect requires hikes through gentle sloping terrain with typical Maine forest cover. The stream itself provides the main collecting area, where weathered material accumulates. Best collecting occurs after spring runoff when new material is exposed but water levels are manageable.

This is a lightly developed site with minimal infrastructure, making it ideal for rockhounders seeking a more remote, authentic prospecting experience. The area has historical significance as part of Maine's smaller-scale mineral exploration heritage, though the prospect is not heavily commercialized.
history: Bemis Stream lies within Maine's productive pegmatite and granite zone, part of the Acadian orogeny that formed during the Devonian period when tectonic collisions created extensive intrusive igneous activity. These granite bodies, particularly those enriched in pegmatitic phases, naturally concentrated minerals like quartz, feldspar, and mica that collectors seek today.
tips: Search along the stream banks and in the alluvial material where water has concentrated lighter mineral fragments - look for translucent quartz chunks and white feldspar shards. Bring a small pry bar or chisel to test exposed bedrock faces on the stream cutbanks, which often reveal fresh crystal faces. Look for areas where the stream has undercut banks; these naturally eroding zones expose the best specimens. Visit after heavy spring rains when new material is exposed and water flow has subsided to safe levels.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,659 feet with gentle slopes typical of the upper Androscoggin drainage. Mixed deciduous and coniferous forest with exposed rocky outcrops and stream-cut banks. Walking distance from parking is variable depending on where stream access is gained; expect 0.5 to 2 miles of hiking through soft forest floor and rocky terrain. Footing is moderately challenging with slippery stream rocks and uneven forest floor.
written_directions: From Township D, follow local roads north toward the Bemis area. The prospect is accessed via stream drainages in the upper elevations of the Androscoggin River watershed. Secondary roads in the area are maintained seasonally; four-wheel drive or high-clearance vehicle recommended. Confirm current road conditions locally before traveling, as Maine's backcountry roads are subject to seasonal closure and washout. GPS coordinates 44.824144, -70.716848 mark the general prospect area. Park at safe pullouts and hike to stream access on foot.
road_conditions: Secondary roads, seasonally maintained, may be rough or washed out in spring. High-clearance or four-wheel drive recommended.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along secondary roads near stream access points. No developed parking area. Space limited, typically 1-2 vehicles.
collection_rules: Collecting is permitted for personal use only on publicly accessible land. Respect private property boundaries. Remove only what you will use or display; do not strip-mine or cause environmental damage. Leave gates as you find them. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
Respect private property
Remove trash
Leave gates as found
nearest_services: Township D and nearby towns approximately 10-15 miles south. Limited services in backcountry area; fuel and supplies should be obtained before traveling to the prospect.
accessibility_notes: Moderate difficulty terrain with stream hiking required. Slippery rocks, uneven forest floor, and elevation gain of several hundred feet from parking areas. Not wheelchair accessible. Sturdy waterproof hiking boots strongly recommended. Stream crossings may be necessary depending on water levels.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Bemis Stream Prospect in Maine?
faq.answer: The primary specimens at Bemis Stream Prospect are quartz crystals, feldspar (often white plagioclase), and mica flakes. These minerals are naturally weathered from the granite and pegmatite bedrock that makes up the western Maine highlands. Quartz is the most common and collectible find here, ranging from small translucent fragments to larger specimen-quality chunks.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Bemis Stream Prospect free and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Bemis Stream Prospect is free with no permit required for personal use collection on public land. There are no fees or special permits needed to visit and collect minerals. However, always respect private property boundaries and check with local authorities about current road access before traveling to this remote site.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding near Township D Maine?
faq.answer: The best season for Bemis Stream Prospect is May through September when weather is mild and hiking conditions are safest. Late May through July offers the most stable conditions. Avoid spring (April-May) when water levels are dangerously high from snowmelt and stream crossings are hazardous, and avoid late fall and winter when roads become impassable and temperatures drop below freezing.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Bemis Stream Prospect?
faq.answer: Bring a small pry bar or cold chisel to test exposed bedrock faces along stream cutbanks where the best specimens hide. A backpack, sturdy waterproof hiking boots, and a headlamp are essential given the remote location and stream terrain. Pack a rock hammer, magnifying glass, and collecting bag. Bring plenty of water and a map; cell service is spotty in this backcountry area.

faq.question: Is Bemis Stream Prospect good for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Bemis Stream Prospect is suitable for beginners and families with children ages 8 and up who are comfortable with moderate hiking. The minerals found here (quartz and feldspar) are easily recognized, and the gentle slopes make access manageable. However, the stream terrain can be slippery, and the remote location requires careful planning. Experienced rockhounds will appreciate the lightly visited prospect where specimens are still available for discovery.

faq.question: How do you get to Bemis Stream Prospect from Rangeley Maine?
faq.answer: From Rangeley (approximately 18 miles south), follow local secondary roads north toward Township D and the upper Androscoggin River drainage where the prospect is located. High-clearance or four-wheel drive vehicles are recommended for the rough secondary forest roads. GPS coordinates 44.824144, -70.716848 mark the area. Confirm current road conditions locally before traveling, as backcountry roads in Maine are seasonally maintained and subject to washout. Park at informal pullouts and hike to stream access on foot.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Bemis Stream Prospect in Maine. Free public land rockhounding near Rangeley. Best May-September.
